questions.question_text: In Alabama single agency, which set of duties does the licensee owe to the client?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties including loyalty and confidentiality
distractors: Only honesty and good faith obligations
Limited duties of disclosure and accounting only
Duties determined by the qualifying broker alone
questions.explanation: Single agency in Alabama means the licensee owes full fiduciary duties to the client: loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, and reasonable care. This is the highest level of duty a licensee can owe and distinguishes client relationships from customer relationships.

questions.question_text: A licensee begins showing properties to a buyer who has not signed a brokerage services agreement. Under Alabama's RECAD, what relationship exists between the licensee and the buyer?
distractors: Single agent for the buyer
Dual agent for the buyer and the seller
Subagent of the listing broker
questions.explanation: Act 2025-380, effective October 1, 2025, removed subagency from RECAD, leaving single agent, dual agent, and transaction facilitator as the permissible relationships. In the absence of a signed brokerage agreement, the transaction facilitator relationship remains in effect by default.

questions.question_text: A single agent learns that the client's property has a structural defect. Which fiduciary duty requires the agent to inform the client?
distractors: The duty of obedience
The duty of accounting
The duty of loyalty
questions.explanation: The duty of disclosure requires the agent to reveal all material facts and information that could affect the client's decisions. A structural defect is a material fact the client needs to know. While loyalty and other duties also apply in single agency, disclosure is the specific duty that mandates sharing this information.

questions.question_text: Which fiduciary duty requires a single agent to properly handle client funds and maintain accurate records?
distractors: The duty of loyalty
The duty of reasonable care
The duty of obedience
questions.explanation: The duty of accounting requires the agent to properly handle all funds entrusted to them and maintain accurate records of financial transactions related to the client's property. This includes earnest money deposits and any other funds received on behalf of the client.

questions.question_text: A seller's single agent learns that the buyer would be willing to pay more than the listed price. What must the agent do with this information?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose it to the seller, the agent's client
distractors: Keep it confidential to be fair to the buyer
Share it with the buyer and the seller equally
Use it only with the buyer's written permission
questions.explanation: A single agent owes fiduciary duties to the client and must disclose material information, and the buyer's willingness to pay more than the listed price is material to the seller. A dual agent, by contrast, could not reveal this, because a dual agent must keep each party's confidences. The single agent's loyalty runs to the client alone.

questions.question_text: What is required before a licensee may act as a dual agent in Alabama?
distractors: Verbal agreement from both parties
Commission approval on a case-by-case basis
Only the qualifying broker's written permission
questions.explanation: Dual agency in Alabama requires informed written consent from ALL parties to the transaction. Verbal agreements are insufficient. The consent must be documented in writing before the licensee can represent both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction.

questions.question_text: Under Act 2025-380, effective October 1, 2025, when does dual agency apply in Alabama?
questions.correct_answer: When one agent represents both buyer and seller
distractors: When two agents in the same firm represent each side
Whenever buyer and seller use the same brokerage
Only in commercial transactions over $500,000
questions.explanation: Under Act 2025-380, effective October 1, 2025, dual agency in Alabama means ONE agent representing both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction. The qualifying broker may designate licensees in writing as designated single agents, and when two designated licensees each represent one party, that is designated single agency, not dual agency. The designation is permissive and must be in writing; it is not automatic.

questions.question_text: Two licensees work under the same qualifying broker, who has designated each of them in writing as a designated single agent. One represents the seller and the other represents the buyer in the same transaction. What type of agency relationship exists?
distractors: Dual agency requiring consent from both parties
Transaction facilitator relationship for both parties
Subagency on behalf of the seller
questions.explanation: Because the qualifying broker has designated each licensee in writing as a designated single agent, this is designated single agency. Each licensee owes full fiduciary duties to their own client. Under Act 2025-380, this is NOT dual agency because two different agents are involved, each exclusively representing one party.

questions.question_text: A dual agent in Alabama receives a confidential offer strategy from the buyer. Can the agent share this with the seller?
questions.correct_answer: No, a dual agent cannot advocate for one over the other
distractors: Yes, the duty of disclosure requires sharing all information
Only if the seller specifically requests it in writing
Yes, both parties consented to full information sharing
questions.explanation: A dual agent in Alabama, acting with the informed written consent of both parties, cannot advocate for one party over the other and must keep each party's confidences. Sharing the buyer's confidential offer strategy would give the seller an unfair advantage. The agent must maintain neutrality between both parties despite representing both.

questions.question_text: In designated single agency, what level of duty does each designated licensee owe to their assigned client?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties to their own client
distractors: Limited duties similar to a transaction facilitator relationship
Shared fiduciary duties with the other designated agent
Only honesty and good faith to both parties
questions.explanation: In designated single agency, each designated licensee owes full fiduciary duties (loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, reasonable care) exclusively to their own assigned client. This preserves the fiduciary relationship even when both agents work under the same qualifying broker.

questions.question_text: When a licensee acts as a transaction facilitator, what is the relationship between the licensee and the parties?
questions.correct_answer: The parties are customers, not clients
distractors: Both parties are clients with full fiduciary rights
The buyer is a client and the seller is a customer
The relationship depends on who pays the commission
questions.explanation: In a transaction facilitator relationship (formerly called transaction brokerage), the licensee assists both parties as customers, not clients. There is no fiduciary relationship with either party. The transaction facilitator must treat all parties honestly, in good faith, and without favoritism, but does not owe the elevated fiduciary duties that come with a client relationship.

questions.question_text: A transaction facilitator learns that a buyer is desperate to close quickly and would accept unfavorable terms. What obligation does the facilitator have regarding this information?
questions.correct_answer: The facilitator must not share it or favor either party
distractors: Disclose it to the seller as a material fact
Share it with both parties for full transparency
Report it to the qualifying broker for guidance
questions.explanation: A transaction facilitator (formerly transaction broker) must treat all parties honestly, in good faith, and without favoritism. Sharing one party's vulnerabilities with the other would constitute favoritism. Since there is no fiduciary relationship, there is no duty to disclose a party's negotiating weaknesses to the other side.

questions.question_text: What is the key distinction between a client and a customer in Alabama real estate?
questions.correct_answer: Clients are owed fiduciary duties; customers receive the statutory duties owed to all parties
distractors: Clients pay commission; customers do not
Clients sign contracts; customers use verbal agreements
Clients are buyers; customers are sellers
questions.explanation: The fundamental distinction in Alabama real estate is the level of duty owed. Clients are owed full fiduciary duties (loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, reasonable care) through a single agency or designated agency relationship. Customers, such as parties assisted by a transaction facilitator (formerly transaction broker), still receive the statutory duties owed to all parties: honesty and good faith, reasonable care, confidentiality, accounting, timely presentation of offers, and disclosure of the licensee's personal interest.

questions.question_text: A seller asks a transaction facilitator to recommend a listing price that will outperform competing properties. How should the facilitator respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline, as advocating for one party is not permitted
distractors: Provide the advice as part of standard brokerage services
Offer the advice only with the buyer's written consent
Provide the advice but also help the buyer negotiate down
questions.explanation: A transaction facilitator (formerly transaction broker) cannot advocate for one party over the other. Providing strategic pricing advice designed to give the seller a competitive advantage goes beyond the facilitator's role of treating all parties without favoritism. This type of advocacy is reserved for agents in a fiduciary client relationship.

questions.question_text: A transaction facilitator discovers a known roof leak during a property showing. Must the facilitator disclose this to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, honesty requires disclosing known defects
distractors: No, the facilitator has no fiduciary duty to the buyer
Only if the seller authorizes the disclosure
Only if the buyer asks about the roof condition
questions.explanation: Although a transaction facilitator (formerly transaction broker) does not owe fiduciary duties, the obligation to treat parties honestly and in good faith requires disclosure of known material defects like a roof leak. Honesty and good faith are baseline duties owed in all Alabama real estate relationships, regardless of the relationship type.
